bonuses.leadin: G.S. Fraser’s translation of this poem opens by asking “Who or what is crying, if not only the wind, at this hour / Only, of uttermost diamonds?” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem written in 512 lines of rhymed alexandrine couplets by the author of “The Cemetery by the Sea”. Its 1917 publication broke its author’s nearly-twenty-year “Great Silence” after the death of Stéphane Mallarmé.
Both “La Jeune Parque” and “The Cemetery by the Sea” were written by this last major French symbolist, whose poem “Palme” inspired James Merrill’s own poem Lost in Translation.

Valery wrote a series of essays about this character, an extremely dedicated intellectual whose motto “Que peut un homme?” means “Of what is man capable?”. This character also claims that “stupidity is not my strong suit”.
answers_sanitized: "La Jeune Parque" [or "The Youngest Parca"; or "The Youngest of the Fates"]
Edmond Teste [or Monsieur Teste; or Mr. Teste]
